title: How silence can be the loudest trigger of all

htmlContent:
The silence that screams

I was sitting in my therapist's office a few years back. She was doing what good therapists do, waiting. Letting the silence stretch. And I felt my chest start to tighten, my throat close up, my hands go cold. I wanted to bolt. I wanted to scream. The silence felt like an attack.

Here's the thing. For most people, silence is peaceful. It's a break from noise, a chance to rest. But for those of us with complex trauma, silence can be the loudest trigger of all. It's not empty. It's filled with everything we've been running from.

I remember being a kid and learning that silence meant danger. When my father went quiet, it meant the explosion was coming. When my mother stopped talking, it meant I'd done something wrong. Silence wasn't a void. It was a warning. My nervous system learned to scan for it, to brace for it, to dread it.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not just in the memory. So when silence hits, your body doesn't think about it. Your body reacts. Your heart rate spikes. Your muscles tense. Your breath gets shallow. You're not remembering the past. You're reliving it, right there in the quiet.

And that's what makes silence so insidious. It's invisible. No one else can see the war happening inside you. They just see you sitting there, looking uncomfortable. They don't know that the silence is screaming at you.

What your nervous system is actually doing

Let's get into the science for a second. Stephen Porges developed Polyvagal Theory, which explains how our nervous system responds to safety and danger. When silence triggers you, your nervous system is doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's detecting a threat and mobilizing a response.

But here's the problem. The threat isn't real. Not anymore. The silence in the room isn't the same silence from your childhood. But your nervous system doesn't know the difference. It's still running the old program. It's still trying to protect you from a danger that passed years ago.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

Think about it. When you're triggered by silence, you might feel the urge to fill it. To talk, to make noise, to distract. That's your sympathetic nervous system kicking in, the fight or flight response. Or you might freeze, go numb, dissociate. That's your dorsal vagal system taking over, the shutdown response. Both are survival strategies. Both are trying to keep you safe.

I've had moments where silence felt so unbearable that I'd put on music, turn on the TV, call someone, anything to break it. I thought I was just being social. But really, I was trying to escape a trigger I didn't even recognize. I was running from my own nervous system.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk walks through exactly how trauma gets stored in the body and why triggers like silence hit so hard. It's not an easy read, but it's a validating one.

Why the wellness industry gets this wrong

I see so much advice out there about silence. Meditate. Sit in stillness. Find your inner peace. And honestly, that advice can do more harm than good for trauma survivors.

Look, I'm not saying meditation is bad. I'm saying that telling someone whose nervous system perceives silence as a threat to just sit in silence is like telling someone with a broken leg to run a marathon. It's not helpful. It's actually re-traumatizing.

When I first tried meditation, I couldn't last thirty seconds. I'd sit down, close my eyes, and within moments I'd be flooded with anxiety, memories, physical sensations. I thought I was doing it wrong. I thought I was broken. But I wasn't. My nervous system was doing exactly what it was supposed to do. It was protecting me.

The wellness industry loves to sell us on the idea that silence is healing. And it can be, for some people, at some stages. But if you're still in survival mode, silence isn't healing. It's threatening. It's a space where all the unprocessed trauma can surface, and if you don't have the tools to handle that, it can overwhelm you.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

I've learned to approach silence differently now. Not as something to conquer, but as something to approach slowly, with respect. I don't force myself into long silences. I start with thirty seconds. Then a minute. Then two. I keep my eyes open. I focus on something safe in the room. I let my body know that this silence is different. This silence is not dangerous.

How to start making silence safe again

So what do you do if silence triggers you? You don't just white-knuckle your way through it. That doesn't work. You have to work with your nervous system, not against it.

First, recognize that your reaction to silence is not a character flaw. It's not a sign that you're weak or broken. It's a sign that your nervous system learned something important. It learned that silence meant danger. And it's trying to keep you alive. Thank it for that. Seriously. Acknowledge that it did its job.

Then, start small. Really small. Don't try to sit in silence for twenty minutes. Try ten seconds. Try five. Then gradually build up. And do it in a context that feels safe. Maybe with a friend in the room. Maybe with a pet. Maybe with a weighted blanket. Give your nervous system something to hold onto.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work is incredible for understanding why different parts of you react differently to triggers like silence.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's book has a whole section on emotional flashbacks and how they get triggered by things like silence. It's practical, grounded, and doesn't pretend recovery is linear.

I also want to say this. It's okay to use noise as a tool while you're healing. It's okay to sleep with a fan on. It's okay to listen to podcasts while you clean. It's okay to not be ready for silence yet. You don't have to prove anything to anyone. Your recovery is yours.

Let's talk about sensory triggers specifically. The smell of a certain cologne. The sound of a particular song. The texture of a fabric. The quality of light at a certain time of day. These aren't "just" memories. They're stored in the most primitive parts of your brain - the amygdala, the olfactory bulb, the thalamus - and they bypass all rational processing.

Bessel van der Kolk's brain imaging studies showed this clearly. When trauma survivors are exposed to triggers, the Broca's area - the part of the brain responsible for speech - goes offline. Literally. The language center shuts down. That's why you can't explain what's happening when you're triggered. That's why "use your words" is such an impossible demand in that state. There are no words available. Only sensation.

If this resonates with you, I want you to know something. You're not crazy. You're not weak. You're not making it up. Your brain is doing exactly what it was designed to do - protect you from threats it has encountered before. The work isn't to stop the triggering. The work is to help your nervous system update its files. To learn that the smell, the sound, the texture - they belong to then. And then is over. Even if your body hasn't gotten the memo yet.

But eventually, you might find that silence starts to change. It becomes less threatening. It becomes a space where you can actually hear yourself, not the trauma, but the real you underneath. That's a long way off for many of us. And that's okay.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson explains why silence in childhood often meant emotional neglect or abandonment, and why that pattern gets wired into our nervous systems.

What silence really asks of us

Silence isn't asking you to be calm. It's asking you to be present. And presence, for trauma survivors, is the hardest thing in the world. Because being present means being in your body. Being present means feeling what you feel. Being present means not escaping.

I still struggle with silence. Some days I can sit with it for ten minutes. Other days I can't handle ten seconds. And I've learned to be okay with that. Recovery isn't a straight line. It's a messy, backward, forward, sideways kind of thing.

Wild, right? The thing that everyone says will bring you peace can feel like the thing that will destroy you. But that's trauma for you. It twists everything. It makes safety feel dangerous and danger feel normal.

So if silence triggers you, I see you. I know how lonely that can feel. I know how confusing it is when everyone else seems to find peace in the quiet and you find only panic. You're not broken. You're not doing it wrong. You're just carrying something heavy. And that weight is real.

Stay with me here. The goal isn't to never be triggered by silence again. The goal is to be able to notice the trigger, name it, and choose how you respond. That's where the healing happens. Not in the absence of triggers, but in the space between the trigger and your response.

Silence might always have an edge for you. It might always carry a trace of the past. But over time, that edge can dull. That trace can fade. And one day, maybe, you'll find yourself in a quiet room and realize you're not afraid anymore. And that will be a victory worth every second of the struggle.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
